creator: i+fbsd@amlegion.org
text:
Jens K. Loewe:

SBCL continues to run on machines despite core count. Just builds flake. Regardless, if built elsewhere, then installed on "aging virtual machine" that works I guess. I think it would be an issue if you rely on building ports locally, vs building elsewhere and installing at the server.

Basically trying to say running it anywhere should basically work, no idea if the same should apply to ability to build it anywhere (otherwise, chromium should be possible to be built on machines with 2gb of ram and 1 core with no swap...).

Lorenzo Salvadore:

I do not believe THREADS should be disabled by default. This would mean that anyone installing from FreeBSD's package repo's would get a version of SBCL without threads. The problem isn't threading in general, as that would continue working at any machine. The problem is building with threading enabled, and FreeBSD's build servers can handle doing so. It would again, using chromium as an example, be like telling chromium to build without multi-tab support due to incapability to build on lesser machines by default. This would in turn cripple the chromium package in the FreeBSD repos for everyone (we're pretending such an option exists for means of comparison.

All:
The solution for this seems to be that building take place on a capable machine. Same as building chromium need take place on a capable machine. No need to cripple the package by default for all users of the package.
